title: Reconstruction-Based Anomaly Detection
concept: Autoencoders trained only on normal images learn to compress and reconstruct normal patterns well, but struggle to reconstruct anomalous inputs accurately — the reconstruction error itself becomes the anomaly signal.

exercises.prompt: Recap: Why can't a one-class model usually explain the specific root cause of a flagged defect?

options.text: It only detects that an input falls outside the normal boundary, without inherently identifying the specific type of deviation
It has no way to process any images at all
It automatically deletes flagged images before analysis

exercises.explain: Yes! Boundary-based flagging alone doesn't explain the specific cause.

cards.title: A forger who's only ever seen genuine bills
cards.body: Imagine an art forger trained to reproduce paintings, but who has ONLY ever studied genuine masterpieces. When asked to reproduce a genuine masterpiece, they nail it perfectly. But asked to reproduce a badly forged fake, their reproduction comes out oddly wrong — because they've never learned how to recreate 'wrong' features.

cards.title: Reconstruction-Based Detection (Autoencoders)
cards.body: An autoencoder is trained ONLY on normal images to compress them into a small representation, then reconstruct them back to the original. Trained only on normal data, it becomes excellent at reconstructing normal patterns but POOR at reconstructing anomalies — the reconstruction error (how different the output is from the input) becomes the anomaly score.

cards.title: A musician who only learned one song perfectly
cards.body: A musician trained to perfectly replay ONE specific song by ear will play it flawlessly. But ask them to replay a completely different, unfamiliar song, and their attempt comes out garbled and wrong — the mismatch between what they tried to play and the real unfamiliar song reveals it wasn't the song they know.

cards.title: Fabric defect detection
cards.body: An autoencoder trained only on images of flawless fabric learns to compress and perfectly reconstruct flawless fabric patterns. Fed a photo of fabric with a tear, it tries to reconstruct 'flawless fabric' from the input, producing a reconstruction that clearly differs from the actual torn input — that difference (reconstruction error) is high exactly where the tear is.

cards.title: High reconstruction error doesn't always mean 'defect'
cards.body: It's a myth that reconstruction error is a perfect anomaly signal. Sometimes normal-but-rare variations (unusual lighting, an uncommon but valid product angle) can also produce higher reconstruction error, causing false alarms — thresholds must be carefully tuned, and this technique works best alongside human review for borderline cases.

cards.title: Reconstructing Normal vs. Anomalous Input
cards.body: What happens when the autoencoder meets each type of input:

left.text:
Autoencoder reconstructs it accurately
LOW reconstruction error
Classified as normal

right.text:
Autoencoder struggles to reconstruct it
HIGH reconstruction error
Classified as anomalous

exercises.prompt: What signal does a reconstruction-based autoencoder use to flag anomalies?

options.text: The reconstruction error — how different the autoencoder's output is from the original input
The exact file size of the input image
The number of bounding boxes detected
The color histogram alone, ignoring reconstruction entirely

exercises.explain: Correct! High reconstruction error signals the input doesn't match learned normal patterns.

exercises.prompt: True or false: An autoencoder trained only on normal images typically reconstructs anomalous inputs just as accurately as normal ones.
exercises.explain: False. It struggles to accurately reconstruct anomalous inputs precisely because it never learned those patterns, producing high reconstruction error.

exercises.sentence: The difference between an autoencoder's output and its original input is called the reconstruction ___.
exercises.explain: Reconstruction error quantifies how well the autoencoder rebuilt the input, serving as the anomaly score.

exercises.prompt: Why might an unusual-but-valid lighting condition cause a false alarm in a reconstruction-based anomaly detector?

options.text: Rare but normal variations can also produce higher reconstruction error, since the autoencoder saw fewer examples of them during training
Lighting conditions are completely ignored by autoencoders
False alarms are mathematically impossible in reconstruction-based detection

exercises.explain: Correct! Reconstruction error isn't a perfectly clean signal — rare-but-normal cases can trigger false positives too.

exercises.prompt: A factory deploys a reconstruction-based anomaly detector for inspecting glass bottles. It suddenly starts flagging many bottles as anomalous right after the factory switches to a new (but still acceptable) bottle cap color. What is the most likely cause?

options.text: The autoencoder was never trained on the new cap color, so it reconstructs it poorly, producing high reconstruction error for a normal-but-unfamiliar variation
The new cap color physically breaks the camera sensor
Reconstruction-based detectors cannot process colored objects at all
This behavior indicates the bottles are genuinely all defective

exercises.explain: Exactly! Unfamiliar-but-valid variations can trigger false positives — a key limitation to plan around. Next: applying this to real manufacturing defect detection workflows.
